Promising Blockchain Pilot Programs in Supply Chain
===================================================

![Image 3: blockchain-pilot-programs-in-supply-chain](https://innovationatwork.ieee.org/wp-content/uploads/2019/07/bigstock-Businessman-Manager-Touching-I-261973714_1024X684.png)

According to [Gartner](https://www.gartner.com/smarterwithgartner/why-blockchain-matters-to-supply-chain-executives/), a leading research and advisory company, [blockchain](https://innovationatwork.ieee.org/blockchain-resources/) has the potential to become a viable solution for major supply chain issues as the complexity of the [supply chain](https://innovationatwork.ieee.org/shipping-giants-employ-blockchain-technology-to-manage-supply-chain-logistics/) sector continues to grow. These issues include counterfeit products, visibility/traceability, and efficiency play. Several companies worldwide are already testing blockchain pilot programs for supply chain in various industries.

Adoption at scale may be at least a decade away. However, Gartner advises that chief [supply chain](https://innovationatwork.ieee.org/5-ways-manufacturers-are-using-blockchain-for-their-supply-chains/) officers begin considering potential applications of blockchain technology and holding formal discussions with their colleagues sooner rather than later.

#### Blockchain in Retail

Following the implementation of a blockchain-based tracking system, Carrefour, a large French retailer, saw sales increase. The blockchain tracking system that the company implemented allows retail customers to track twenty of the company’s products, such as meat, milk, and fruit, through the supply chain, starting at the farm all the way to Carrefour stores. Because of its initial success, Carrefour is planning to add 100 additional products to the system later this year, including non-food items.

U.S.-based retailers are also exploring blockchain applications. Walmart has made headlines with its blockchain [use cases](https://innovationatwork.ieee.org/creating-a-blockchain-use-case-in-7-steps/), and [Target](https://www.coindesk.com/retail-giant-target-is-working-on-a-blockchain-for-supply-chains) is currently developing a [blockchain-powered supply chain](http://innovationatwork.ieee.org/how-blockchain-is-transforming-the-supply-chain/) management solution called ConsenSource.

Since mid-2018, Target has been working on a blockchain proof-of-concept that tracks the certification of Target’s paper manufacturing suppliers. In a [blog post](https://tech.target.com/2019/04/22/exploring-the-benefits-of-blockchain.html), Joel Crabb, Target vice president of architecture, wrote that the company has been “working directly with forest managers and certification boards” to identify what data is available and can be shared on a distributed ledger.

The blog also states, “Many companies— including Target— see the most potential for enterprise blockchain initiatives as open source. Open-source projects require all participating parties to define the governance model collectively from the outset, so companies can then focus their time working on blockchain-based solutions that will lead to greater speed, transparency and cost savings.”

Specific details on the proof-of-concept are still under wraps. In its last annual report to shareholders, Target did say that it is working on supply chain improvements. However, it doesn’t directly mention blockchain or distributed ledger technology.

#### Pilot Programs

Retail giants are not alone in their exploration of blockchain pilot programs. Panalpina, one of the world’s largest transport and logistics companies, has started cautiously testing a variety of possible applications of blockchain technology. The Swiss company has launched two blockchain pilot programs in supply chain, one investigating blockchain applications in high-tech industrial goods while the other deals with office supplies.

The company’s goal is to optimize supply chains by digitizing trade documents, storing them in the cloud, and deploying blockchain technology. This method should improve processes while reducing long term costs. Panalpina’s system, built on blockchain, will track goods imported from Asia to Europe. In order to test the system, it is set up to run in parallel with existing processes. This will allow real shipments to continue without disruption of current processes.

“These early-stage projects are 85 percent about digitization and 15 percent about blockchain– we are starting to see clear benefits in cost savings through simplified and speedier processes, and lower document courier costs. But the real advantage of blockchain lies in the ‘single source of truth.’ Improved data sharing between trade partners creates more transparency, with clear ownership and responsibility for each documented step in the supply chain,” says Cedric Rutishauser, senior venture development manager at the Panalpina Digital Hub.

#### More than Supply Chain

Supply chain is not the only field that can benefit from distributed ledger technology. Blockchain also has the potential to alter and disrupt a wide range of other sectors. Learn more about the future of this transformative technology with IEEE’s [Enterprise Blockchain for Healthcare, IoT, Energy, and Supply Chain](https://forms1.ieee.org/Enterprise-Blockchain-Course-Program.html?LT=EA_WB_IAW_20190709_BC_Article) course program. Ideal for managers, professional engineers, and business leaders, this advanced course program is designed to provide blockchain use cases across key industries and sectors.

[To learn more about bringing this program to your organization, connect with an IEEE content specialist today!](https://forms1.ieee.org/Enterprise-Blockchain-Course-Program.html?LT=EA_WB_IAW_20190709_BC_Article)

# **The Next Chapter in Fresh Food Logistics**

The pivotal change in supply chain demands transparency and consistent efficiency. Keeping that in mind, forward-looking companies are embracing **supply chain transformation** by integrating blockchain and AI. These are not theoretical concepts or proposed next-gen tech to make an impression on the industry. Instead, they are actively being piloted across the agri-food sector with real results.

Let’s take a look further in this blog; go through real compelling pilot examples and include hypothetical and informed insights. The insights showcase the role of blockchain pilot projects and AI traceability in redefining the future of fresh produce logistics.

## **IFPA’s Strategy in Action: Driving Supply Chain Transformation**

IFPA is moving towards a new world of food chains, and they encourage others to reimagine beyond the traditional way of managing fresh produce. Fostering the “supply chain of the future” initiative, improve and enhance global collaboration via innovation events like collabthons. The initiative is changing the idea of solving problems, and it is only possible with technological enhancement.

Is there any success? A growing list of **IPFA success stories** has been observed by IFPA. Where stakeholders from producers to retailers are participating in pilot programmes, they are implementing smart contracts apart from sharing data protocols and real-time monitoring.

The main aim after these collaborative pilots is to minimise the food wastage by leveraging AI-powered blockchain. For instance , an agro-based industry using blockchain technology also has those temperature sensors embedded. After this, industry shipments are now being tracked in real time. if there is slight changes in the temperature, then it will trigger actions, including rerouting or priority processing.

It preserves the main goal of **supply chain transformation** to extend the shelf life of products and foster accountability across the value chain. Because tackling spoilage with blockchain will increase the chances of getting higher quality success.

## **Fighting Spoilage, Fueling Supply Chain Transformation**

It has been observed agro industries are facing near 30% loss in fresh produce globally because of the fragmented cold chain management To reduce the graph , companies are experimenting with **Agri-food blockchain** platforms that work on key components like traceability and time-stamped data from farm to retail.

In one such pilot, a realistic case modelled from IFPA’s traceability goals, growers embed IoT sensors in pallets. These sensors calculate and gather the temperature and humidity data, then store it into the blockchain network.

Logistics partners use a real-time dashboard that can verify cold chain compliance at every checkpoint. In case some kind of malicious activity is noticed within the chain or smart contract tool, it will notify the next protocol and trigger insurance protocols. Not only this, but it prevents several other loss like spoilage and the best part is the blame game between suppliers and distributors.

To reduce data silos and make every stakeholder responsible and accountable, a company like Primafelicitas is developing the best possible solution that makes industries’ supply chains secure and scalable.

Their team expertise always goes beyond enterprise-grade standards, ensuring data integrity , verifiable audits and prompt deployment.

[**PrimaFelicitas**](https://www.primafelicitas.com/) is a well-known name in the market, serving worldwide consumers by delivering projects based on Web 3.0 technologies such as **AI, Machine Learning, IoT, and Blockchain**. Our expert team will serve you by turning your great ideas into **innovative solutions.**

## **Smart routing through AI analytics : Fostering Efficiency**

Applying pilots in AI-Powered smart routing reflects a positive impact on supply chain. Unlike traditional legacy systems that often ignore crucial points like the overripe of fresh produce that leads to food wastage. By integrating AI intelligence and decentralised technology, the delivery of products optimises as per the location and freshness.

There is a recent pilot scenario where a distributor was inspired by the Primafelicitas AI solution. The respective distributor caters berries around california using an AI Model into its supply chain system. These AI models gather consistent knowledge from historical data, weather and shelf life as per that provide accurate predictions.

These data points are paired with blockchain-validated time stamps. further, the system rerouted the delivery destination dynamically to transit the freshest product.

This **AI Traceability Case Study** represents technological change from logistics tasks into a strategic advantage and ensures equilibrium between supply and demand. The final result establishes a new way of collaboration among stakeholders: growers , shippers and retailers.

## **Data Privacy With Pellucidity : Secure Sharing Via Smart Escrow**

One of the most revolutionary and underrated use cases of blockchain is smart data escrow. The complete ideology revolves around maintaining harmonized standards. Stakeholders are hesitant while exchanging the information where competitive concerns are a second thought.

But **agri-food blockchain** emphasises a smart data escrow system that empowers stakeholders to connect with others without losing control of proprietary insights. Here’s how it works in a [**Blockchain Pilot Project**](https://www.primafelicitas.com/blockchain/future-of-blockchain-innovations/)**.** For instance, let’s take an example of a retailer who wants to verify whether a supplier or grower is using relevant growing practices but doesn’t need to have other information about the supplier’s operations.

Next thing is the supplier can store data securely on a blockchain and set conditions to access the data via the smart contract method. The retailer’s system can get access to the required data fields and receive cryptographic proof of compliance, not more than that.

To develop a sense of accountability and responsibility , the blockchain company “primafelictas” engineers a smart contract system in such a way that implementations are seamless. Their systems enable granular data permissioning that drives complete trust and transparency together within supply chain.

In whole-building collaborative ecosystems where transformation can embrace a new age of fresh produce chains.

## **Dynamic Incentives: Decreasing Quantity, Enhancing Quality Assurance**

Supply chain is always into producing volume without keeping the purity a priority. Because it was always about making more markets across retailers and earning huge amounts of money. Rather developing a sense of responsibility and delivering authentic values towards the market.

It automatically led to more wastage; inventory management got disturbed, and health issues and environmental impact were the bigger drawbacks in supply chain. But making the system trustworthy becomes a responsibility, so to do so, IFPA introduces the dynamic incentives concept.

In simple terms, every contributor in fresh produce supply chain gets rewarded on the basis of the condition and quality of output. Further, that is verified by blockchain and AI.

There is a company dealing with the shipment of “Avocados” and their duty is to keep the product in excellent condition. Further, AI-powered blockchain gives quality assurance, as per that data point logged immutably within the blockchain.

The grower whose datasets represent quality gets paid with premium pricing; on the other hand, lower-quality suppliers receive baseline pricing. This creates a smart mechanism which rewards best practices in harvesting, storage and shipping as per the market standards.

Such innovation gives the world a clarity about **supply chain transformation** that firmly aligns with the agro-industries vision and values. Such innovations is a proportionate mixture of both AI and blockchain; further, they make sure that from producer to consumer, products are in their best possible condition.

## **Latin America’s Innovation Hub for Supply Chain Transformation**

Supply chain transformation takes fresh produce industry from pilot to practice. They are now fostering innovation hubs , a collaborative channel where stakeholders test, refine and scale new systems. These hubs are helping some of the global retailers; let’s take an example from a success story where large groups of retailers are connecting with smallholder farms across Latin America.

The stakeholder co-develops a shared traceability system and makes a unified standard of system that keeps data for transparent collaboration. It reduces the cost by eliminating extra infrastructure maintained by farmers.

Whole productions run under a blockchain that keeps data integrity and AI flags inconsistencies and predicts delays. This is an important part of a real-world **blockchain pilot project** that turns fragmented supply chains into interconnected and transparent networks.

## **Conclusion**

From intelligent routing and spoilage/contamination reduction to smart incentive systems and secure data collaboration. Various **blockchain pilot projects** are emerging across the produce industry; it is pointing towards a future which fosters two different technical developments ( Blockchain and AI).

The fusion has already come so far, from papers to processing real field operations. The successful deployment of pilot projects results in resilience and open doors towards smart logistics. The early wins are already laying a foundation for scalable, real-world supply chain transformation.

Using blockchain to advance minerals traceability with LuNa Smelter
===================================================================

November 2021

 • 

 share  Share 

Featured technology

Blockchain

QR Codes

Who we’re helping

Our supply chain partners

Our role

Partnered with Cisco, SGS, Volkswagen, Minsur, Minespider, the Responsible Minerals Initiative, LuNa Smelter, and the Rwanda Mines, Petroleum, and Gas Board to launch a blockhain pilot to improve transparency and accountability across supply chains

![Image 1: Image of a QR code on shipments from the mining site](https://www.gstatic.com/marketing-cms/assets/images/9c/1f/bc4779f040b7a80dc354d228584f/4-1.webp=w950-h550)

Nearly every electronic device we use contains tin. It acts as the glue that holds our smartphones and other consumer electronics together. But how does it get there? Pinpointing the mines and smelters that tin comes from—and all the steps it passes through—can be a challenge.

That’s because mineral supply chains have grown increasingly complex in nature—and this complexity can make it more difficult to trace every part and material throughout its full life cycle. This is especially true in remote areas, where information is often difficult to come by.

Blockchain technology enables a broad set of stakeholders to navigate these issues and improve transparency and accountability across their supply chains.

[The first phase of our blockchain pilot](https://sustainability.google/operating-sustainably/stories/traceability/)—a joint effort between Google, Cisco, SGS, Volkswagen, and Minsur—took place in 2018 at Minsur’s San Rafael mine in Peru. In 2020, we partnered with Berlin-based startup Minespider; the Responsible Minerals Initiative (RMI); LuNa Smelter in Rwanda; and the Rwanda Mines, Petroleum, and Gas Board on the second phase of the pilot for end-to-end traceability in Rwanda’s Great Lakes Region. LuNa Smelter is a leading producer of high-quality, responsible tin, and it’s the only tin smelter in Africa conformant with the RMI’s Responsible Minerals Assurance Process (RMAP).

Each company involved is part of the RMI ecosystem—Google, Cisco, and Volkswagen are active members of the RMI; Minsur and Luna are RMAP Conformant smelters; and SGS is an RMI-approved audit firm. Using a blockchain protocol from Minespider, the pilot helped track minerals at every touchpoint on the value chain, from mine to consumer.

Building on the lessons learned in Peru, the Rwandan pilot had a few key objectives. First, evaluate Minespider’s blockchain-based OreSource tool as a way to help smelters and mines more easily meet global minerals regulations, including the new European Union regulations. Second, test the feasibility of having different digital traceability tools exchange information, ensuring interoperability and data privacy across multiple platforms. And third, demonstrate that blockchain traceability can scale to include not just large established mines like Minsur’s in San Rafael but also small-scale artisanal mines that have fewer resources.

“Tools like OreSource can benefit responsible mines and have a positive impact on targeted communities by creating a direct link between upstream and downstream stakeholders,” says Aleksandra Cholewa, a supervisory board member of LuNa Smelter.

RMI helps bring many of these pieces together. “The RMI fully supports traceability of materials through the value chain—and the inclusion of artisanal, small-scale mines is critical,” says Marianna Smirnova, the RMI’s director of Standards and Assurance. “The RMI is planning to provide an additional layer of support by including the RMAP conformant status on the OreSource blockchain.”

![Image 2: Staff at work at LuNa Smelter in Kigali, Rwanda](https://www.gstatic.com/marketing-cms/assets/images/a0/03/b78360f648f3b9e64d1ae9c72eaf/unnamed1.jpeg)

 Ingot production at LuNa Smelter in Kigali, Rwanda. 

**Mineral sourcing regulations meet new technology**

Mines where tin and other metals are extracted must meet exacting responsible sourcing regulations. Notably, the European Union enacted its Conflict Minerals Regulation in 2021. The approach is similar to the Dodd–Frank Wall Street Reform and Consumer Protection Act, passed in 2010, that governs publicly traded companies operating in the United States.

These safeguards are intended to keep supply chains free from non-conformant conflict minerals and reduce human rights abuses, including occupational health and safety issues, forced labor, community rights violations, and environmental degradation.

Building these safeguards and conducting strong due diligence is an imperative that can present challenges: Suppliers can be discouraged by counterproductive levies and the need to present extensive documentation to meet the regulations. The high costs associated with due diligence efforts may marginalize small-scale miners.

Enter blockchain. Best known for its association with cryptocurrency, blockchain ledger technology offers a decentralized way to record supply chain transactions that is verifiable, tamperproof, permanent, and publicly accessible. It can also add layers of protection for data and intellectual property. Phase two of the OreSource pilot tested the potential of blockchain tools to offer mines, smelters, and traders a simpler way to meet sourcing regulations.

Minespider founder Nathan Williams likens the technology to a Russian nesting doll. The data uploaded into the blockchain includes a private layer of invoices, chain-of-custody documents, and other sensitive information that’s visible only to the next organization in the supply chain. Others in the supply chain can confirm the existence of the documentation and request it directly from the owner. Companies can also upload spreadsheets, videos, and other documentation to authenticate their sourcing.

“This data is valuable but has not been traditionally shared along the supply chain,” says Williams. “And once you include three or four companies, keeping track of it becomes very complex.”

When LuNa is ready to ship its tin or products containing tin, it uses OreSource to print a QR code that’s then attached to the shipment. Downstream inspectors scan the code to access the data for a complete picture of the tin’s journey.

Cholewa says the printed QR codes show the details of the shipment, weight and quality of the material, number of ingots, and the smelter’s conformance status. The QR codes also can reveal the origin of minerals used for production and data on individual mining sites and communities. In the next phase of the project, LuNa will use lasers to engrave the QR codes directly onto the metal.

**Traceability is always the top priority**

LuNa was a natural fit for the project’s second phase. Acquired and refurbished in 2018 by Luma Holding, LuNa has strong environmental and social responsibility credentials, with a focus on improving gender equality and developing underserved communities. When the company set up in Rwanda, LuNa made due diligence—in particular digital traceability—one of its first concerns.

![Image 3: Staff at LuNa Smelter in Kigali, Rwanda, process ore into ingots of tin](https://www.gstatic.com/marketing-cms/assets/images/27/10/3a00babc44dda7da6fc33480cda0/unnamed3.jpeg)

 Staff at LuNa Smelter in Kigali, Rwanda, process ore into ingots of tin. 

“We are strongly committed to best practices in the industry and to sourcing responsibly,” says Cholewa. “The due diligence of our own supply chain is always the top priority for us.” Participating in the pilot is now helping LuNa take its due diligence to the next level.

In the future, the blockchain may contain records on every gram of material, as well as data on each mining site, including smaller mines.

“It’s important to cover all of the players with the traceability umbrella, especially in Africa,” Cholewa says. “There is a significant artisanal mining community that cannot be neglected. It has to actively participate in that process.”

**Connecting smaller companies with bigger supply chains**

Larger companies with bigger budgets and more know-how have an easier time complying with mineral-sourcing regulations, says Cholewa. Solutions like Minespider are leveling the playing field.

Many responsible small-scale artisanal mines located in conflict-affected areas don’t have the means to demonstrate that their metals were responsibly sourced. Blockchain technology can help dispel these concerns and provide the necessary information to connect these mines to bigger supply chains.

“What we’re trying to do here is make the process more transparent and include critical due diligence information, not make it a financial burden,” says Williams.

He says the next step is to get OreSource in as many mines as possible, particularly smaller ones, to see how well it scales. From there, he says, it will be used for other metals, such as cobalt and gold. Williams says interest is high.

LuNa has already brought more than 200 tons of tin from Rwanda into global markets, using QR codes generated by Minespider to trace its journey. “I think that we are showing how to do responsible sourcing, and that it’s possible to do it,” said Cholewa. “It’s a whole new world for tin, and we are happy to be a part of this.”

Highlights
----------

*   •Blockchain enhances security, transparency, and efficiency through tamper-proofed records. 
*   •It transforms industries like transportation, manufacturing, food, and healthcare by making them more efficient and accountable. 
*   •Blockchain improves traceability and provenance throughout complex supply chains. 
*   •It addresses problems like counterfeiting, managing suppliers, and sustainable sourcing. 
*   •Smart contracts and IoT integration bring more supply chain functionality to blockchain. 

Abstract
--------

Blockchain technology is emerging as one of the most transformative forces to have ever reshaped supply chain management, with unique transparency, security, and efficiency. This paper reviews the literature on blockchain technology and applications in core industries such as transportation, manufacturing, food and beverages, and healthcare. At its core, blockchain deploys distributed ledger technology to provide tamper-proof, secure record-keeping, enhancing traceability and provenance verification for complex supply chains. Smart contracts, IoT connectivity, and decentralized financial services allow blockchain to address its most critical challenges, including counterfeiting, supplier management, and the enforcement of sustainable and responsible sourcing practices. However, widespread adoption of blockchain in supply chains is inhibited by severe issues like scalability, interoperability, regulatory uncertainty, and lack of standardization. Additionally, the environmental impact of blockchain, i.e., the energy-intensive proof-of-work mechanisms is examined, along with potential strategies for mitigation. As the technology continues to evolve, the integration of artificial intelligence and 5G networks will further reshape supply chain management, unleashing new efficiencies and capabilities.

1. Introduction
---------------

A blockchain is a chain of blocks, serving both as a repository of information and as a secured ledger for transactions. Each block keeps a list of recent transactions, and once the block is complete, then it becomes a part of the permanent blockchain. The database updates with each new block that comes along, allows for secure money, property, and contract transfers. In a nutshell, blockchain is a software protocol operating over the Internet. This technology ushers in a new economy whereby blockchain will rule over digital assets, currencies, and smart contracts [[1](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2772503025000192#bib0001)]. The various application of blockchain range from healthcare and banking to the public sector and are therefore an active concern of many studies and implementations. For instance, in the health sector, it has provided a safe foundation for storage and record-keeping of patient information, thus allowing the sharing of confidential data among medical experts. The blockchain safeguards the security of financial products [[2](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2772503025000192#bib0004)]. Many organizations and enterprises are interested in how the use of blockchain fits into their operations in order to improve their performance and gain a competitive edge. Increased breakthroughs in technology will definitely translate to increased usage in more areas of application. Blockchain enables secure and non-repudiable data storage and management; hence, it is regarded as a critical building block in the digital economy. In any case, some of the most revolutionary technologies, such as blockchain, are very likely in the future to turn a number of industries tune to secure, transparent, and decentralized networks [[3](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2772503025000192#bib0005)].

Blockchain disrupted conventional financial systems by enhancement, remittances, smart contracts, among other things through a decentralized ledger system [[4](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2772503025000192#bib0006)]. A new cryptocurrency asset classes have also evolved on blockchains, hence disrupting rather well-established financial systems. In the case of supply chain management, blockchain presents considerable application in enhancements in traceability and transparency in transaction and asset records. Tamper-proof record creation enables the tracking of goods in real time, reducing counterfeiting, as well as improving the efficiency within the supply chain as a whole [[5](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2772503025000192#bib0056)]. In the healthcare sector, blockchain has promised further development in managing patient data, clinical trials, and pharmaceutical supply chains which can help patients to take full control over their medical information, while researchers will benefit from improved data accuracy and integrity through secure interoperable data sharing. It also finds applications in peer-to-peer energy trading, carbon credit systems, and grid management. Energy platforms based on blockchain allow for direct transactions among the producers and consumers of energy. This directly translates to increased utilization of renewable energy, reduced dependence on monopolized power systems, and generally a big cost saver [[5](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2772503025000192#bib0056)]. [Fig. 1](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2772503025000192#fig0001) shows the decision making and interaction framework involving policymakers, producers, and users. The green and yellow colours represent each player's two scheduling and decision-making agents [[6](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2772503025000192#bib0011)].

Blockchain technology is also explored in other sectors such as real estate, government services and intellectual property rights, adopting higher standards of transparency, minimal fraud, and convenience to the processes. The main barriers to mainstream usage are the constraints of scaling, interoperability, legal frameworks, and energy consumption. Several barriers have to be traversed so as to reach the full utilization of blockchain technology in business operations. Based on type, it could be classified in the four categories i.e., public blockchains, consortium blockchains, private blockchains, and hybrid blockchains as shown in [Fig. 2](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2772503025000192#fig0002).

![Image 12: Fig 2](https://ars.els-cdn.com/content/image/1-s2.0-S2772503025000192-gr2.jpg)

Fig. 2. Types of blockchains.

The public blockchain is stored decentralized so that it is not under the control of any single entity. This is non-possessive in nature and every participant who owns required hardware along with access to internet can join the network. Every computer in the network keeps an accurate copy of the whole blockchain, and every bit of information is available transparently. In every network block, there is a replica present on each computer to make sure that any dataset is completely distributed, tamper-proof, and transparent across the whole network. Public blockchains form the backbone for cryptocurrencies like Bitcoin and Ethereum because they allow openness in participation and also in the details of transactions [[7](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2772503025000192#bib0022)]. Contrary to public blockchain, private blockchains are restricted in their accessibility. They can be accessed and participated by those persons or entities that are authorized. These types of blockchains are present with a controlled environment to keep sensitive data. Due to limited participation, private blockchain networks provide more privacy and quick transaction processing than public blockchains. These are typically utilized in industries that require a high level of control and privacy, making them well-suited for applications in banking and supply chain management [[8](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2772503025000192#bib0052)].

Hybrid blockchains incorporate aspects of both public and private blockchains. While there may be a central entity in control of the network-perhaps even to ensure that unauthorized changes do not occur, the blockchain can nonetheless be open to the public for transparency. This blockchain model is flexible, hence allowing the organization to take advantage of the strengths between the public and private models. While the public side ensures transparency, the private side should keep in close confidence, with confidentiality over sensitive data guaranteed [[9](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2772503025000192#bib0020)]. The integration of IoT and blockchain in supply chains enhances transparency, security, and efficiency through the capability to trace goods in real-time, enable automated payments, and store safe data. IoT sensors monitor the location and condition of goods, while blockchain ensures data integrity. Smart contract-based automatization of payments and activities reduces delays. RFID and GPS facilitated by IoT enhance inventory management, while predictive analytics optimize logistics. This fusion eradicates fraud, counterfeiting, and regulatory non-compliance, ensuring the authenticity and sustainability of the product. Ultimately, IoT-driven blockchain supply chains create a secure, automated, and efficient ecosystem. Due to the continuous advancements, blockchain technology enhances risk management and supply chain resilience, rendering decentralized data sharing in a more efficient and secure way [[10](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2772503025000192#bib0031)]. In blockchain, there is always trade-off between time, cost, and quality [[11](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2772503025000192#bib0034)]. Blockchain and crypto-based IoT supply chain networks enhance security and efficacy [[12](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2772503025000192#bib0035)]. An open innovation paradigm through blockchain is leveraged in formulating data-led supply chain frameworks that advance transparency as well as defend against fraud [[13](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2772503025000192#bib0032)]. Blockchain is also crucial in the health sector, demonstrating its efficiency in safe waste disposal and ensuring robustness and sustainability in healthcare networks. Applications of this kind make blockchain a reliable, decentralized option for industries that are most critical [[13](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2772503025000192#bib0032)].

The consortium blockchain model, also known as Federated Blockchain, introduces collaboration by allowing multiple entities to jointly hold the reins of control for the blockchain. A consortium blockchain is neither public nor private; it is run by a set of pre-selected group members. It finds the best fit in those use cases that need the collaboration of industries, such as supply chain, finance, and healthcare. It allows a trade-off between transparency and control and therefore is a more efficient means of meeting collective needs for a diverse set of stakeholders [[14](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2772503025000192#bib0044)]. A comparison among these blockchain technologies is listed in [Table 1](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2772503025000192#tbl0001).

Table 1. Blockchain technologies.

| Blockchain type | Advantages | Disadvantages | Use cases | Examples |
| --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |
| **Public** | Reliable and Secure, Decentralized and Anonymity, and Immutable and transparent | Slow transaction, High Energy Consumption, Acceptance Issues | Traditional financial systems, decentralized applications (dApps) | Bitcoin, Ethereum, Litecoin |
| **Private** | High Speed, Scalability, Privacy & permissioned access. | Security concerns, Centralized control, Trust issues (Required trust in centre Authority) | Voting Systems, Internal Auditing, Supply Chain Management | Hyperledger, Corda |
| **Hybrid** | Ecosystem-driven, Lower Costs, Combined benefits of public and private block chains, Balanced Operations between public and private attributes | Efficiency can be limited, Lack of full transparency, Managing the ecosystem can be complex | Healthcare, government service, real estate, and financial institutions | Ripple network, XRP token |
| **Consortium** | High speed transaction, Privacy, Efficient for industry collaboration | Requires approval from consortium members, Transparency concerns, Limited Public Access | Enterprise Solution, and payment processing | Quorum, Multichain |

Blockchain technologies have a compromise between security and transparency, with public blockchain offering transparency but scalability and energy concerns, against efficiency of private and consortium types but lacking decentralization. Hybrid and federated blockchains attempt to balance both but introduce governance complexity. Barriers to adoption are framed in terms of regulatory hurdles, trust in central authority, and multi-party governance complexities.

2. Application of blockchain techniques in industries
-----------------------------------------------------

Different sectors have adopted blockchain technology with innovations in both design and applications. It facilitates cross-border payments in finance and banking, helps with the execution of smart contracts in a manner that increases the levels of efficiency and reduces time, supports secure management of electronic health records in healthcare, and enhances the integrity of clinical trials. In real estate, it finds use in property title management and in tokenizing real estate for fractional ownership, enabling easier transactions. [Fig. 6](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2772503025000192#fig0006) shows the exponential growth in the application of blockchain technology [[15](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2772503025000192#bib0013)]. In the healthcare sector, blockchain improves clinical trials by enhancing reproducibility and transparency through the recording of trial data and verification of results [[16](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2772503025000192#bib0037)]. This ensures transparency and accuracy are maintained in research processes. In intellectual property, blockchain provides a secure mechanism for digital rights management. It ensures fair compensation for creators and protects their intellectual property, hence fostering trust and fairness [[17](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2772503025000192#bib0019)]. The gaming and entertainment industry benefits from blockchain through the use of smart contracts, which guarantee royalty payments and enable fair income sharing. Blockchain also allows players to own and exchange digital assets outside gaming systems, enhancing ownership and flexibility [[18](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2772503025000192#bib0038)]. In the case of voting and governance, blockchain ensures decentralized governance, which opens the decision-making process and ensures more transparency. Additionally, blockchain allows for secure voting with traceability, reducing fraud within a supply chain, as mentioned by [[19](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2772503025000192#bib0046)].

# Blockchain in Supply Chain Management: Benefits, Use Cases, and Global Insights

Key Takeaways

## Global Market Overview and Expansion

As global supply chains grow more complex, blockchain is becoming a key driver of enterprise transformation. Businesses worldwide are adopting it to build trust, automate processes, and achieve full traceability across every stage of the supply network.

### The Role of Blockchain in Modern Supply Chains

Blockchain acts as a distributed trust layer, enabling real-time validation of inventory, movement, and compliance data across multi-party ecosystems. Its immutable records eliminate reconciliation delays and enable synchronized visibility across all stakeholders.

McKinsey projects a 45% efficiency improvement in digital supply chain transformation using blockchain-led processes, particularly in pharma, retail, and automotive sectors.

### Global Adoption Trends Across Industries

[The blockchain supply chain market](https://www.grandviewresearch.com/industry-analysis/blockchain-supply-chain-market-report) is expected to reach $192.93 billion by 2030, growing at a CAGR of 88.8% from 2024 to 2030. Furthermore, blockchain use cases are expanding rapidly, including agri-trade traceability, smart compliance in pharmaceuticals, and counterfeit prevention in the luxury goods sector.

Infosys notes a 60% YoY growth in pilots moving to production systems globally, especially among Tier 1 suppliers integrating with blockchain-powered ERPs.

![Blockchain Supply Chain Market Size](https://appwrk.com/wp-content/uploads/2025/07/dfhdhdshsdhsdh-1024x655.webp)

### **Investment and Innovation Highlights**

Major VC-backed startups like Everledger, Chronicled, and VeChain have secured multi-million dollar rounds to support blockchain deployment in logistics, [food traceability](https://appwrk.com/industries/food-and-beverages-software-development/fb-traceability-software-development), and pharma authentication. Governments in the UAE, Singapore, and the EU are also driving pilot funding.

## How Blockchain Improves Supply Chain Operations: Key Benefits and Real-World Use Cases

Blockchain technology is transforming supply chain operations by enhancing transparency, traceability, and operational efficiency. From preventing counterfeits to streamlining audits and automating payments, the use cases and benefits mentioned below demonstrate how blockchain adds trust and value across every supply chain layer.

### Healthy Customer Relationships: Building Trust Through Provenance and Accountability

Blockchain in supply chain management helps build customer trust by showing exactly where a product comes from and how it moves through the supply chain. When consumers scan a product, they can see verified information about its origin, safety checks, and delivery history.

This transparency builds confidence and holds all parties accountable, especially in industries like luxury goods, food, and pharmaceuticals.

### Strengthened Traceability: Securing Product Provenance with Immutable Records

It goes without saying that reliable traceability is essential for a secure and transparent supply chain. Blockchain strengthens this by recording every product movement in real time on an unchangeable digital ledger. This is especially valuable in food safety, where quickly identifying the source and transit path can prevent contamination issues.

It also plays a key role in protecting high-value products from counterfeiting.

### Enhanced Security: Protecting Supply Chain Data from Breaches and Manipulation

Using smart contracts and permissioned blockchain architecture, blockchain secures critical supply chain data. The immutable recordkeeping ensures encryption-backed authenticity while reducing the risks of cyber breaches. The result is not only compliance-grade encryption but also robust, secure data management that ensures tamper-resistance and regulatory alignment.

Each transaction in the blockchain-powered supply chain ecosystem is validated and encrypted, protecting sensitive documents and operational logs from manipulation.

### Real-Time Tracking and Transparency: Enhancing End-to-End Supply Chain Visibility

Blockchain in supply chain enables real-time tracking across **multi-tier supplier visibility** networks using decentralized ledger technology (DLT). With immutable ledger entries and tamper-proof data logging, stakeholders gain full supply chain visibility, from manufacturing units to the last-mile delivery. This digital ledger transparency ensures that every touchpoint is auditable, fostering trust and efficiency across the decentralized supply chain ecosystem.

### Reduced Costs and Higher ROI: Automating Operations and Reducing Middlemen

Blockchain benefits the supply chain by eliminating intermediaries through [smart contracts](https://appwrk.com/case-studies/smart-contract-development-tnft). These self-executing protocols reduce paperwork, speed up settlements, and cut overhead costs associated with traditional supplier negotiations. The result: measurable cost reduction and accelerated ROI, especially in payment workflows and invoice reconciliation.

**Also Read**: [How Much Does ERP Software Development Cost – 2025 Guide](https://appwrk.com/insights/erp-software-development-cost)

### Faster Processing: Accelerating Approvals, Payments, and Audits

Blockchain speeds up supply chain processes by making critical documents and approvals instantly accessible to all parties. This includes customs forms, inspection records, and supplier verifications. By reducing wait times and improving visibility, it supports faster decision-making and keeps inventory moving efficiently through just-in-time operations.

### Better Quality Control and Compliance: Automating ESG, Safety, and Regulatory Reporting

Blockchain improves quality control and compliance by automatically recording important data like safety checks, sourcing practices, and environmental impact. Smart contracts help enforce rules and trigger actions, such as inspections or reports, without manual effort. This makes it easier for companies to follow ESG guidelines and prevent counterfeiting, especially in industries with strict regulations.

### Sustainable and Energy-Efficient Supply Chains: Blockchain for ESG Tracking and Environmental Audits

Blockchain is becoming a key tool for building greener, more responsible supply chains. It securely records data on energy use, emissions, and sourcing practices through smart sensors, helping companies monitor their environmental impact in real time. This tamper-proof system supports accurate carbon tracking, easier ESG reporting, and stronger compliance with global sustainability standards.

### Cybersecurity and Tamper-Proof Data Control: Securing Supply Chains with Immutable Audit Trails

Supply chains face growing risks from ransomware, data breaches, and insider threats. Blockchain enhances security by decentralizing data access and using permissioned blockchain frameworks. This model eliminates single points of failure, ensures immutable recordkeeping, and enforces identity-verified access to mission-critical supply chain events.

### Interoperability and Platform Integration: Connecting ERP and SCM Platforms via Blockchain

Modern SCM systems must integrate with blockchain seamlessly to ensure operability across finance, procurement, and logistics nodes. Seamless **blockchain ERP integration** ensures synchronized data flow between procurement, inventory, and logistics systems, eliminating silos and manual errors. Distributed ledger technology supports interoperability by syncing with [ERP](https://appwrk.com/services/erp-development-services), CRM, and IoT endpoints. ## Future of Blockchain in Supply Chain: Trends & Predictions

The future of blockchain in supply chains is headed toward smarter integration with emerging technologies, greater automation, and stronger ESG transparency. These trends are set to redefine global logistics over the next few years.

### Greater IoT Integration

Combining [IoT](https://appwrk.com/iot-mobile-apps-the-future-of-smart-devices) and blockchain allows companies to track goods in real time, including their temperature, location, and condition. This creates a seamless connection between physical items and their digital records, improving accuracy and control across the supply chain.

By 2026, 60% of global container shipping is expected to use IoT sensors linked to blockchain for route optimization and compliance logging.

### Advancements in AI and Machine Learning

AI will help businesses analyze trusted data stored on the blockchain to predict issues, spot fraud, and adjust pricing in real time.

Machine learning models trained on secure and unchangeable data can make more accurate forecasts and rate suppliers more effectively.

### Sustainability and Ethical Sourcing

As environmental and social regulations grow stricter, blockchain will help verify carbon footprints, recyclable materials, and fair labor practices. It will also play a key role in supporting climate reporting and proving that businesses are sourcing materials responsibly.

Major fashion, electronics, and agri brands are already embedding ethical sourcing modules for EU compliance in 2026. Global retailers now use **blockchain for ethical sourcing certifications** to authenticate labor conditions, fair trade standards, and sustainable material sourcing.

### Rise of New Business Models

Decentralized [supply chain systems](https://appwrk.com/industries/supply-chain-management-software-development) will emerge, where suppliers, buyers, and financiers interact via tokenized contracts.

This includes micro-invoicing, cross-border smart trade, and supplier funding tied to on-chain risk profiles. With **tokenized supply chain assets**, suppliers and logistics partners can digitize cargo units for real-time tracking, financing, and smart escrow payments.

### Increased Transparency and Automation

Smart contracts are expected to automate 70-80% of key operational workflows such as purchase approvals, trade confirmations, shipping validation, and compliance checks. This will not only speed up execution but will also ensure accuracy and accountability at every step.

Blockchain’s role will shift from record-keeping to **operational orchestration**, especially in multi-region logistics.

## Blockchain Adoption by Region: USA, UK, EU & APAC Trends

Different regions are embracing blockchain in supply chains based on their unique business goals and regulatory environments. While Europe focuses on compliance and traceability, the US and Asia-Pacific are leading with innovation-driven pilots and tech-first implementations.

### Blockchain Adoption in the USA

The US leads in enterprise-scale blockchain deployment, especially in pharma (DSCSA compliance), food traceability (IBM Food Trust), and defense logistics. Amazon, Walmart, and FedEx have each launched blockchain pilots to streamline freight tracking, verify sourcing, and automate payments.

### Supply Chain Innovation in Australia

Australia’s government support for blockchain has sped up its use in agriculture and logistics. A pilot project by the Australian Trade and Investment Commission and VeChain improved cattle export tracking by 38%, especially for shipments to China.

Ports in Brisbane and Melbourne are now testing blockchain with IoT to track and verify containers in real time.

### Compliance and Data in the UK

UK-based logistics firms use blockchain primarily for customs compliance and post-Brexit traceability. Regulated industries, notably aerospace and [healthcare](https://appwrk.com/best-healthcare-mobile-app-development-company), have integrated smart contracts to automate cross-border checks.

The FCA’s sandbox environment has also enabled rapid blockchain trials in supply chain finance.

### Traceability Advances in Europe

The EU’s Green Deal has made blockchain-powered ESG tracking mandatory for certain industries by 2026. BMW, Carrefour, and Nestlé are running active traceability programs using Hyperledger and Ethereum-based frameworks. Furthermore, blockchain is being used to trace CO2 impact across product lifecycles, especially in textiles, agri-food, and electronics.

## Challenges of Blockchain in Supply Chains: Integration, Privacy, Legal Risks

While blockchain brings major benefits to supply chains, successful implementation requires careful planning. Businesses need to address compatibility with legacy systems, protect sensitive data, meet regulatory requirements, and ensure the solution can scale with operations.

### Integration with Legacy Systems

Most enterprises still run on legacy ERPs, WMS, or standalone logistics platforms. Blockchain doesn’t replace existing systems. It connects with them through APIs or middleware, but without proper integration planning, it can lead to data silos or duplicated records.

### Data Privacy and Cybersecurity Risks

Protecting sensitive business data on blockchain can be difficult, especially when using public networks without proper encryption or access control. Meeting privacy regulations like GDPR also adds complexity, often requiring companies to store certain information off-chain or use advanced methods to keep user data secure.

### Interoperability and Scalability

Effective blockchain adoption in supply chains depends on seamless integration across multiple platforms and stakeholders. Without interoperability, systems remain fragmented, and data becomes trapped in isolated silos, reducing overall efficiency.

Although solutions like Polkadot and Cosmos are advancing cross-chain communication, enterprise-grade integration is still developing. Scalability also remains a concern, as public blockchains such as Ethereum have faced limitations under high transaction volumes; an issue that Layer 2 technologies like Polygon are now working to address.

### Legal and Regulatory Uncertainty

Laws governing smart contracts, digital signatures, and blockchain records vary globally. Enforceability remains jurisdiction-dependent, making global contract automation risky unless local legal mapping is done first.

### Energy Consumption and Environmental Concerns

As sustainability becomes a top priority, the environmental impact of blockchain technology is under increased scrutiny. Traditional proof-of-work blockchains like Bitcoin consume large amounts of energy, but most modern supply chain solutions now use energy-efficient alternatives such as proof-of-stake or permissioned networks.

For ESG-focused companies, it’s essential to assess blockchain’s energy footprint across infrastructure, validation methods, and data storage layers.

## Cost Savings and Risk Mitigation with Blockchain in Supply Chain Management

Blockchain in supply chain delivers measurable ROI by reducing errors, cutting reconciliation costs, and minimizing fraud risk. Organizations use blockchain inventory optimization to balance stock levels across warehouses based on verified, real-time demand and fulfillment data.

### Reduced Costs and Errors

Blockchain reduces manual errors and data mismatches across procurement, inventory, and payments by embedding a single source of truth. Capgemini reports that it can cut data reconciliation costs by up to 70%.

### Streamlining Payment Processing

Smart contracts enable instant payments as soon as delivery is confirmed or an inspection is complete. This removes delays, reduces the need for middlemen, and is especially useful for global supply chains where fast, secure payments are critical.

### Risk Reduction Through Transparency

Blockchain offers tamper-proof records of supply chain activity. This improves risk profiling, helps enforce contracts, and supports stronger terms in trade finance and insurance.

## Blockchain vs. Traditional Systems in Supply Chain Management

Traditional supply chains rely on paper forms, email-based approvals, and siloed ERP databases, creating friction, manual overhead, and fraud risk. Blockchain removes these inefficiencies by creating **shared, tamper-proof ledgers** that enable real-time, trustless collaboration.

|  |  |  |
| --- | --- | --- |
| **Feature/Functionality** | **Traditional Systems** | **Blockchain-Enabled Supply Chains** |
| **Data Sharing** | Data is shared manually, and access is limited to a few parties. | All parties have real-time access to shared and secure information. |
| **Trust Verification** | Trust is established through third-party intermediaries. | Trust is built through consensus mechanisms and smart contracts. |
| **Document Audits** | Audits are performed manually and are often delayed. | Audit trails are created instantly and stored securely on the blockchain. |
| **Transaction Speed** | Transactions take several days due to paperwork and manual reviews. | Transactions are completed in minutes through automated smart triggers. |

Companies adopting blockchain see up to 40% faster inventory cycles, 30% fewer disputes, and 70% less reconciliation time.

## How to Integrate Blockchain into Your Supply Chain: A Strategic Guide

To unlock full value, the blockchain integration roadmap must align with strategic supply chain objectives, involve partner coordination, and include early-stage system planning.

### Identify Key Use Cases

Blockchain is most valuable where multiple parties need to share data, traceability is lacking, payments are delayed, or audits are complex. Focus on a single high-friction process, such as inventory reconciliation or customs compliance, to start small and scale effectively.

### Choose the Right Blockchain Network

The choice between **public, private, and consortium blockchains** affects security, cost, and interoperability.

Your industry’s regulatory posture also impacts network selection.

### Collaborate with Partners

Blockchain works best when everyone in the supply chain is involved. To get real value, suppliers, carriers, and logistics partners need to join the network, which requires clear standards, easy onboarding, and agreed-upon rules for how the system is managed.
